---
title: "Cost and Schedule Risk, CPM Replay, On Your Own Machine"
description: "A register-driven Monte Carlo platform for project contingency: Excel in front, a Python engine behind, full CPM replay for schedule risk, and no data leaving the building. All numbers from a synthetic showcase project."
author: "Steve Parton"
date: "2026-07-04"
categories: [Risk Analysis, Monte Carlo, QRA, SRA, Project Finance, Claude, Vibe Analytics]
draft: true
---

*Everything shown in this post comes from a synthetic showcase
project, a wind farm that exists only as a test fixture. The method
is exactly what runs on client engagements; the numbers are not. It is however all based on recent experiences just in a different industry*

## The gap

To run quantitative risk analyses (QRA) and schedule risk analyses (SRA) on a mid-sized capital project, you're choice of approaches is 
your options are thin. The tool much of the industry grew up on has
not had a major release in over a decade. The credible replacement
is priced and positioned for megaproject portfolios. Newer
cloud platforms are impressive, but they want your schedule uploaded
to their servers and priced as a percentage of project value, and
their distributions come from historical benchmarks rather than your
project's own risk register.

So most projects between $25M and a few hundred million get a static
spreadsheet register and a P80 someone once calculated. Government
business cases in Australia still require probabilistic contingency
at exactly this scale, so a bit of an issue.

So Claude and I rolled our own.

<!-- IMG-1: dashboard PM overview page, synthetic 4-period arc -->

## What it is

An integrated cost and schedule risk solution that runs on a laptop that handles:
 - the initial QRA/SRA prior to project start.
 - the ongoing active QRA/SRA management during construction.

The risk register is in Excel comprising usual information about risk identification, mitigation, quantitative and schedule assessments with a short cut QRA using the 
Excel sits in front: the risk register, the parameters, the
dashboard your steering committee reads. A Python engine sits
behind it: 100,000 (or whatever) Monte Carlo iterations, vectorised, seeded,
reproducible to the bit. Between them, a contract of named ranges,
so the analyst updates assumptions in a familiar workbook and never
touches code.

The risks come from a facilitated workshop and live in a register
with distributions your reviewers can interrogate line by line. That
is a deliberate methodological position: an auditor, a lender's
technical adviser or a funding department can walk from any P-value
back to a named risk, an elicited range and a documented rationale.
No black box, no benchmark blend.

<!-- IMG-2: Excel workbook dashboard sheet, synthetic wind project -->

## Schedule risk without proxies

Cost Monte Carlo is well-trodden. Schedule risk is where methods
diverge. The common shortcut classifies each risk's network effect
(series, parallel, somewhere between) and applies a multiplier to
convert duration impact to cost. It is fast, explainable, and for
many purposes defensible. It is also a proxy: nothing actually
happens to the critical path.

This platform started tiered, with the multiplier approach as the
first pass, and then we built the full thing: a CPM replay engine.
Every iteration fires its sampled risks, perturbs the affected
activities, walks the entire Primavera network forward (the parser
and kernel handle networks of thousands of activities and logic
links), and reads the finish date the schedule itself produces. The
delay is whatever the critical path says it is.

The validation result is the part worth a paragraph. When we ran the
same register through both methods, the multiplier approach and the
CPM replay produced P-values within touching distance of each other,
with the replay showing a slightly fuller tail, exactly where you
would expect a proxy to under-read. Two independent methods agreeing
is what defensibility looks like in practice: the fast method is
validated for triage, the replay method carries the number that
matters, and the comparison itself goes in the report. Criticality
metrics follow AACE 65R-11, so the reviewer sees a standard they
recognise.

<!-- IMG-3: tornado / criticality chart, engine-generated PNG from synthetic run -->
<!-- IMG-4: cost-schedule S-curve overlay, engine-generated PNG from synthetic run -->

## The unglamorous parts are the product

Two war stories, because detail like this is the difference between
a prototype and something you can trust.

Excel lock files. When a workbook is open, Excel plants a ghost file
(the `~$` prefix) and formula cells can quietly read back as empty
to code. Quietly is the problem: no error, wrong inputs. The engine
now runs a pre-flight check that refuses to start if it finds one.

Formula caching. Getting clean values out of a workbook that mixes
hand-entered numbers with calculated cells took six iterations to
get right. The rule that emerged: the engine either reads a value it
can verify or it stops with a named error. It never guesses. That
rule (fail closed, never fabricate) is now enforced across the
platform by more than 1,900 automated tests, golden fixtures that
must reproduce bit-identical results, and a cross-validation gate
that reconciles the Python engine against the Excel deliverable
before anything ships. I built part of the early engine on a boat
off Bali, which says something about the workflow, or my hobbies.

## After the report: the monthly loop

A contingency study is a photograph. Projects move. The platform's
third layer re-runs the full CPM engine each reporting period
against updated actuals and risk states, and publishes to a web
dashboard: stochastic Gantt, drivers, and a what-changed view that
explains why this month's P80 is different from last month's. The
project team maintains the register; the engine and a reviewing
practitioner do the rest. The split matters for adoption: the
register-keeper on the project team needs nothing beyond Excel and a
browser. Python, the engine and the report toolchain belong to the
trained risk analyst who runs the period cycle. The dashboard stays
alive only while the engine keeps feeding it, which is how a
leave-behind should work.

<!-- IMG-5: dashboard stochastic Gantt, synthetic periods -->
<!-- IMG-6: dashboard what-changed / delta view -->

## Where the analyst fits

I have run cost risk on feasibility studies and business cases for
thirty years. What changed is not the method, it is who does the
typing. Claude wrote every line of the Python and the dashboard; my
contribution was the methodology: which distribution for which risk,
how cost and schedule severity should share a draw when one event
hits both, what the AACE standard requires, and when to trust the
number. That division of labour suits an experienced analyst
unreasonably well. The methodology knowledge was always the hard
part. The code was the barrier, and it is gone.

Which is the real point of this post. Tools like this do not replace
the analyst. They move the analyst up a level, the same way
spreadsheets did in the eighties: the computation gets cheap, the
judgment gets valuable, and in a world where anyone can generate an
analysis, the analyst whose numbers can be verified is the one who
gets believed.

## By the numbers (synthetic showcase run)

- Monte Carlo iterations per run: 100,000, seeded, reproducible
- Schedule engine: full CPM replay, networks of thousands of
  activities and links
- Automated tests behind the platform: 1,900+
- Lines of Python written by me: 0
- Data leaving the building: none, it runs on a laptop
- SaaS subscriptions required: 0

If your project needs a contingency number that will survive review,
or your analysts are still doing this by hand, the methodology
conversation is the interesting one.

---

*Steve Parton is a finance and analytics consultant at SJP
Consulting. If you are working in project risk or infrastructure
finance and want to talk methodology, find me on
[LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/in/stephen-parton).*
